company_profile.name: SPX Technologies, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods
company_profile.description: SPX Technologies, Inc. engages in the supply of engineered solutions serving the heating, ventilation, and cooling (HVAC); and detection and measurement markets in the United States, Canada, China, the United Kingdom, and internationally. The company operates in two segments, HVAC and Detection and Measurement. The HVAC segment engineers, designs, manufactures, installs, and services package and process cooling products and engineered air movement and handling solutions for the industrial, institutional, and commercial HVAC markets, as well as hydronic and electrical heating and ventilation products for the residential, industrial, institutional, and commercial markets. It offers cooling products and engineered air movement and handling solutions under the Marley, Recold, SGS, Cincinnati Fan, TAMCO, Ingénia, Air Enterprises, and Rahn Industries brand names; hydronics and electrical heating and ventilation products under the under the Berko, Qmark, Fahrenheat, Leading Edge, Patterson-Kelley, Weil-McLain, Sigma, Omega, Skypeak, Thermolec, Williamson-Thermoflo, INDEECO, Heatrex, AccuTherm, Brasch, Spectrum, BannerDay PipeHeating, and Solar Products brands. The Detection and Measurement segment offers underground pipe and cable locators, inspection and rehabilitation equipment, robotic systems under the Radiodetection, Pearpoint, Schonstedt, Dielectric, Cues, ULC Robotics, and Sensors & Software brands; transportation systems under the Genfare brand; communication technologies under the TCI, ECS, and KTS brands; and aids to navigation products under the Flash Technology, ITL, Sabik Marine, Sealite, and Avlite brands. The company markets its products through consumers, independent manufacturing representatives, third-party distributors, and retailers. The company was formerly known as SPX Corporation and changed its name to SPX Technologies, Inc. in August 2022. SPX Technologies, Inc. is headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina.
company_profile.description_summary:
This company manufactures and services engineered solutions for HVAC systems and detection and measurement equipment across industrial, commercial, and residential markets globally. Revenue primarily comes from its HVAC segment, which produces cooling systems, air handling solutions, and heating products under multiple brand names, while its Detection and Measurement segment offers pipe locators, inspection equipment, robotic systems, and navigation aids.

The company distinguishes itself through an extensive portfolio of established brands across both segments and its integrated approach to serving diverse end markets from residential heating to industrial infrastructure inspection.

company_profile.name: Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile S.A.
company_profile.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
company_profile.description: Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile S.A. produces and sells specialty plant nutrients, and iodine and its derivatives in Chile, Latin America, the Caribbean, Europe, North America, Asia, and internationally. It offers potassium nitrate, sodium nitrate, specialty blends, and other specialty fertilizers under the Ultrasol, Qrop, Speedfol, Allganic, Ultrasoline, Prop, and Prohydric brands. The company also provides iodine and its derivatives for use in medical, agricultural, industrial, and human and animal nutrition products comprising x-ray contrast media, biocides, antiseptics and disinfectants, pharmaceutical intermediates, polarizing films for LCD and LED screens, chemicals, organic compounds, and pigments, as well as added to edible salt to prevent iodine deficiency disorders. In addition, it produces lithium carbonate and lithium hydroxide which are used in the production of cathode material for, secondary batteries; lithium chloride; and basic lithium chemicals and lithium derivatives used in lubricating greases for heat-resistant glass, chips for the ceramic and glazing industry, and air conditioning chemicals, as well as other pharmaceutical syntheses and metal alloys. Further, the company produces potassium sulfate; and potassium chloride which are used to nourish various crops. Additionally, it produces and markets industrial chemicals, such as sodium nitrate mainly used in the production of glass and explosives, metal processing and recycling, and production of insulating materials and adhesives; potassium nitrate used as a raw material to produce frits and special ty glass, as well as in the enamel, metal treatment, and pyrotechnic sectors; solar salts used as a thermal storage medium in solar power generation plants; and potassium chloride as an additive in oil drilling and food processing sectors, as well as sells third-party fertilizers. Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile S.A. was founded in 1926 and is headquartered in Santiago, Chile.
company_profile.description_summary:
This company produces and sells specialty plant nutrients, iodine derivatives, and lithium chemicals globally. Revenue comes primarily from specialty fertilizers like potassium and sodium nitrate sold under multiple brands, alongside iodine products for medical, agricultural, and industrial applications, and lithium compounds for batteries and manufacturing.

The company distinguishes itself through integrated operations across complementary chemical sectors, leveraging Chilean mineral resources to serve diverse end-markets from agriculture to electronics and renewable energy.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. This makes earnings-based methods like P/E unreliable, because the earnings figure at any single point in time can be unusually high or low. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

company_profile.description_summary:

_meta.type_rationale: Bank or thrift — P/Tangible Book prioritised; DCF excluded

Banks also carry a lot of debt by design — they borrow money to lend it out — so high leverage is normal and not a red flag.

_meta.type_rationale: Cyclical business (basic materials) — EV/EBITDA and P/S prioritised over P/E
_meta.method_explanation: Cyclical companies — such as semiconductors, mining, or manufacturing — have profits that rise sharply in good times and fall sharply in downturns. EV/EBITDA smooths out some of this distortion by looking at earnings before interest and tax, making it a fairer comparison across the economic cycle. Price-to-Sales is also useful as revenue tends to be more stable than profits for cyclical businesses.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: STERIS has a moderate competitive moat built on strong profitability and predictable cash flows, but the absence of exceptional returns on invested capital and constrained growth limit its ability to fend off competitors over time.

metric_assessments.profitability: At 13.2%, the profit margin sits in the upper-middle range for industrial healthcare businesses, reflecting solid operational efficiency and pricing discipline. The company is converting revenue into earnings at a respectable rate—typical for mature, well-run businesses, though not exceptional. What's holding profitability back from a higher score is the lack of a visible ROIC figure and the absence of evidence that the company is capturing outsized returns, which would suggest a stronger competitive advantage or unique market position.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: The current ratio of 1.96 is healthy and above the 1.5 threshold that conservative investors look for, meaning STERIS has nearly two dollars of liquid assets for every dollar of short-term obligations. A debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.31 is manageable and typical for mid-sized industrial companies—not a red flag, but not fortress-like either. The company has adequate financial flexibility to invest and weather downturns, though it's not so lightly leveraged that it can weather any conceivable stress with ease.
metric_assessments.growth: Annual revenue growth of 7.3% is steady and respectable for a mature healthcare services company, but it's below the 10-12% that truly high-growth businesses achieve, and well below the 15%+ that young, expanding sectors often post. Earnings-per-share growth of 9.1% over the next five years projects slightly faster gains than revenue, which suggests margin expansion or buybacks are expected to help. The company is explicitly flagged as growth-constrained, meaning management faces real limits on how much it can accelerate expansion—a mature market, established competitors, or regulatory barriers are likely at play.
metric_assessments.predictability: With a score of 8 out of 10, STERIS demonstrates the kind of steady, repeatable earnings power that comes from serving essential healthcare facilities with long-standing contracts. The high predictability score reflects a business model where demand is relatively stable and customer relationships are sticky. What prevents a perfect score is that even predictable businesses can face sector-wide shocks (like supply chain disruptions or regulatory changes), and the growth constraints suggest the company may have limited upside surprises to offer shareholders.
metric_assessments.valuation: At a score of 5 out of 10, the stock appears neither cheap nor expensive in absolute terms—it's fairly priced for what the business delivers. With mid-single-digit revenue growth and solid but unspectacular returns, the market is not giving STERIS a significant premium, but it's also not trading at a deep discount that would compensate for limited expansion potential. An investor paying today's price is betting on steady earnings and dividends, not a windfall from multiple expansion or breakaway growth.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: STERIS is rated Speculative because while it generates reliable profits and maintains solid financial footing, its growth is deliberately constrained and its competitive moat—though real—is not wide enough to guarantee market share gains or pricing power. This is a 'show me' story: the company has proven it can earn money and manage its balance sheet, but investors are taking on meaningful risk that the business won't grow faster than the broader economy or that a stronger competitor could erode margins. For a beginner, this means you'd be buying a steady but slow-growing business at a fair price, with real uncertainty about whether it will outpace inflation and stock market averages over the next five years.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: STERIS is a well-run healthcare business with strong profitability (13.2% margins), reliable earnings predictability, and a solid balance sheet that can support investment without excessive financial stress. However, the company faces meaningful headwinds: its growth is deliberately constrained and running at only 7.3% annually, well below what younger or more dynamic businesses achieve, and its competitive moat, while real, is only moderate—leaving it vulnerable to margin pressure from larger or more innovative rivals. Looking ahead, earnings are expected to grow at 9.1% per year over the next five years, slightly faster than revenue, but this alone is unlikely to excite growth investors or deliver market-beating returns. The stock appears fairly valued at current levels, offering steady cash flows and reasonable dividends but no obvious margin of safety or growth catalyst that would justify taking on the elevated risk of a speculative rating. For most beginners, STERIS is a "show me" story—worth watching as a potential core holding if the company can prove it can accelerate growth, but not compelling enough to buy today without a lower entry price or a credible turnaround plan.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: State Street's strong profitability, high margins, and predictable cash flows from its custody and asset servicing business create a meaningful competitive moat, though limited organic growth and modest returns on equity prevent it from reaching exceptional strength.

metric_assessments.profitability: State Street's 19.6% profit margin is well above the 10–15% range typical of large financial institutions, reflecting disciplined cost management and pricing power in its core custody business. The company extracts strong earnings from every dollar of revenue, which demonstrates operational excellence and the ability to serve clients efficiently. However, the 11.26% return on equity, while respectable, lags the 15–20% threshold associated with truly exceptional capital deployment, suggesting the company's substantial equity base isn't generating outsized returns.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A score of 7/10 reflects a solid balance sheet appropriate for a major custodian and asset manager with stable funding and reasonable leverage. State Street's financial position is adequate to weather downturns and support its operations without material stress. The modest gap to a higher score reflects the inherent leverage typical in financial services—while appropriate for the industry, it means the company's buffer against severe shocks is good rather than exceptional.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue declined 2.8% year-over-year, a headwind that stems from lower trading volumes and subdued asset management activity—both tied to broader market cycles rather than competitive loss. The projected 15.1% earnings growth over five years is substantially driven by cost management and operational leverage rather than top-line expansion, revealing a business constrained by its mature position in a competitive industry. This ceiling on organic growth is the defining weakness holding the company back from higher overall quality.
metric_assessments.predictability: An 8/10 score reflects State Street's highly stable revenue base rooted in long-term client relationships, contractual fee arrangements, and recurring custody and administration services that are relatively insensitive to market volatility. Fee-based income and multi-decade client relationships create earnings streams that are exceptionally predictable compared to trading-dependent or market-sensitive competitors. The slight gap to perfection reflects modest cyclical exposure to asset management flows and occasional revenue pressure during periods of low market activity.
metric_assessments.valuation: A 6/10 score indicates the stock is trading at fair value rather than at a meaningful discount to intrinsic worth. The combination of steady profitability, low growth, and solid market position suggests the current price reflects the company's fundamental strengths and limitations reasonably well. While not cheap enough to offer a margin of safety for a conservative investor, the stock is not overvalued in a way that introduces unnecessary downside risk.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: State Street earned a Safe rating because it combines strong and predictable profitability—evidenced by its 19.6% margins and 8/10 predictability score—with a solid balance sheet and low business volatility. The primary limiting factor is constrained growth; declining revenue and a mature competitive position mean earnings expansion will be modest and largely dependent on cost discipline rather than expanding the business. For a beginner, this means you're buying a reliable, established financial institution that should deliver steady returns but won't surprise you with explosive growth.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: State Street is a fortress-like asset custodian and administrator with exceptional profitability and highly predictable earnings driven by long-term client contracts and recurring fee income—exactly the kind of business quality that appeals to conservative investors seeking stability. The 19.6% profit margins and 8/10 predictability score reflect a company that generates dependable cash flow in all market conditions, which is a genuine strength in an uncertain world. However, the business faces a meaningful headwind: revenue actually declined 2.8% last year, and growth is structurally capped by its mature position in a competitive industry, leaving future earnings gains to come primarily from cost-cutting rather than expanding the customer base or assets under management. At current valuation levels, the stock appears fairly priced with no compelling discount, offering reasonable returns through dividends and modest capital appreciation rather than exceptional upside. For beginner investors seeking a stable, low-drama financial holding with predictable earnings and dividend income, State Street fits the bill—but don't expect it to outpace the market by much.

It offers savings and checking accounts, and time deposits; personal, mortgage, unsecured, and car loans; overdrafts; loans with special facilities for project and working capital financing; and leasing, bank guarantees for tenants, salary advances, domestic and international factoring, international guarantees and letters of credit, payroll payment plans, and credit and debit cards, as well as financial services and investments, such as mutual funds, guarantees, and benefit payments for senior citizens. The company also provides foreign trade and cash management; advisory services; treasury services; insurance products comprising life, home, personal accidents, technology, ATMs, protected bag and content, and integral insurance product for entrepreneurs and SME customers and other insurance policies; and asset management and other services, as well as operates as a digital online broker. It operates through a network of branches, ATMs, and self-service terminals. The company was formerly known as Inversiones y Participaciones S.A. and changed its name to Grupo Supervielle S.A. in November 2008. Grupo Supervielle S.A. was founded in 1887 and is based in Buenos Aires, Argentina.
company_profile.description_summary:
Grupo Supervielle is a financial services holding company operating in Argentina. It generates revenue through personal and business banking, corporate banking, insurance, and asset management. The company offers savings accounts, various loan products, credit and debit cards, mutual funds, and insurance coverage including life and home policies.

The company operates a comprehensive distribution network of branches, ATMs, and self-service terminals, and functions as a digital online broker, providing integrated financial solutions across retail and corporate segments.

_meta.type_rationale: Bank or thrift — P/Tangible Book prioritised; DCF excluded
_meta.method_explanation: Banks make money from the difference between what they charge on loans and what they pay on deposits — not from traditional business cash flows. Because of this, standard cash flow models (DCF) do not work well for banks. Instead, we focus on Price-to-Book (P/B), which compares the stock price to the value of the bank's assets on its books. Banks also carry a lot of debt by design — they borrow money to lend it out — so high leverage is normal and not a red flag.

quality.company_type_note: Banks and REITs carry structural leverage — do not describe financial_strength scores of 7+ as 'moderate concern' for these types.

_notes.revenue_vs_net_income: This chart shows how much money the company brings in (revenue) versus how much it actually keeps as profit (net income) each year. A healthy company should show both lines growing over time. If revenue grows but net income shrinks, the company is spending more than it is earning — a warning sign worth investigating.
_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.

_meta.type_rationale: Standard company — balanced weighting across all methods

company_profile.name: Constellation Brands, Inc.
company_profile.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 22.4%; DCF methods prioritised
company_profile.description: Constellation Brands, Inc., together with its subsidiaries, produces, imports, markets, and sells beer, wine, and spirits in the United States, Canada, Mexico, New Zealand, and Italy. It offers beer under the Corona Extra, Corona Familiar, Corona Sunbrew, Corona Light, Corona Non-Alcoholic, Corona Premier, Modelo Especial, Modelo Chelada, Modelo Negra, Modelo Spiked Aguas Frescas, Modelo Oro, Modelo Noche Especial, Victoria, Vicky Chamoy, and Pacifico brand names. The company also offers wine under the Sea Smoke, Schrader Cellars, Kim Crawford, Mount Veeder, Ruffino, My Favorite Neighbor, Robert Mondavi Winery, and The Prisoner Wine Company brand names; and spirits under the Casa Noble, High West, Mi CAMPO, and Nelson's Green Brier brand names. It provides its products to wholesale distributors, retailers, and state alcohol beverage control agencies. Constellation Brands, Inc. was founded in 1945 and is headquartered in Rochester, New York.
company_profile.description_summary:
This company produces, imports, markets, and sells beer, wine, and spirits across North America, New Zealand, and Italy. Beer generates its primary revenue, with leading brands including Corona and Modelo. It also offers wines under multiple labels and spirits through various subsidiaries, distributing products to wholesalers and retailers.

The company distinguishes itself through a diverse portfolio of premium and popular brands across all three alcohol categories, with particularly strong market positions in beer through Corona and Modelo, which command significant share in their segments.

_meta.type_rationale: FCF compounder — FCF margin 22.4%; DCF methods prioritised
_meta.method_explanation: Free cash flow compounders are businesses that consistently generate more cash than they need to run the business — think of companies like consumer goods brands or software firms with subscription revenue. Because their cash flows are reliable year after year, a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works very well. DCF asks: if I add up all the future cash flows this company will generate and adjust for the time value of money, what is it worth today? For these companies, that calculation gives a meaningful and trustworthy answer.

quality.note: Moat scored by Haiku API — final_score_pct computed out of 50.

quality_narrative.moat_rationale: Constellation's exceptional profitability and 22.64% return on equity demonstrate pricing power and operational excellence in beverage alcohol, though its moderate ROIC of 11% and slowing revenue growth suggest the moat is real but beginning to narrow.

metric_assessments.profitability: An 18.5% profit margin is exceptional—most consumer companies operate in the 5–12% range—showing Constellation converts sales into earnings far more efficiently than peers. The 22.64% return on equity reflects management's ability to squeeze profits from shareholder capital and indicates strong brand recognition and pricing power in beer and wine, where consumers show loyalty to familiar names. However, the 11.01% return on invested capital, while respectable, falls short of the 15–20% that truly elite businesses achieve, revealing that some of the profit gains come from financial leverage rather than pure operational dominance.
metric_assessments.financial_strength: A current ratio of 1.08 and debt-to-EBITDA of 3.31 paint a picture of a company running lean on cash and carrying substantial debt relative to earnings—typical for mature beverage companies but restrictive if business conditions deteriorate. The 3.31x leverage ratio sits above the 2–2.5x comfort zone for most industries, meaning debt service obligations consume a meaningful portion of profits and leave little room for economic shocks or major investments. Constellation is not in financial distress, but the balance sheet lacks the fortress-like strength that would allow aggressive shareholder returns or opportunistic acquisitions during downturns.
metric_assessments.growth: Revenue fell 11.3% year-over-year, a significant contraction that signals softening demand in a competitive beer and spirits market where volume growth has stalled industrywide. The 4.2% forecast for earnings growth over five years is well below historical GDP growth and typical for mature consumer staples, reflecting management's expectation that the business will grow modestly from cost discipline rather than market expansion. The company is explicitly growth-constrained—it cannot rely on expanding the overall market, so future returns will depend on share gains or margin expansion, both of which are difficult to sustain long-term.
metric_assessments.predictability: A 6/10 score reflects the contradiction between Constellation's stable, repeatable business model and the recent revenue weakness that has introduced near-term uncertainty. Beer and wine consumption patterns are historically steady, and the company has demonstrated consistent brand loyalty and pricing power over decades, supporting confidence in baseline earnings. However, the sharp 11.3% revenue decline and the growth-constrained outlook make near-term earnings forecasts less reliable, as management must navigate shifting consumer tastes (away from beer toward spirits and alternatives) and intense retail competition.
metric_assessments.valuation: A 6/10 score indicates the stock trades at a reasonable but not compelling price given current conditions. The company's strong profitability and market position justify a modest premium to the broader market, but the negative revenue trend, high leverage, and modest long-term growth rate mean investors should not expect a significant discount. Fair value depends on whether the revenue decline is cyclical (temporary softness) or structural (permanent shift in preferences); without clarity, paying a full price makes sense but a margin of safety is warranted.

quality_narrative.classification_reason: Constellation earned a Speculative rating because it combines a genuinely profitable and defensible business with significant financial constraints and near-term headwinds. The company's exceptional profit margins and pricing power in beer and wine provide downside protection, but the 11.3% revenue drop, debt load of 3.31x EBITDA, and slow 4.2% long-term earnings growth mean the stock is not a stable, low-risk holding—it requires conviction that management can stabilize sales and gradually delever, making it more suitable for investors comfortable with uncertainty than for conservative portfolios.
quality_narrative.investment_narrative: Constellation is a genuinely profitable business with strong brand moats and pricing power in beverage alcohol, earning an exceptional 18.5% profit margin that shows how effectively it converts sales into cash. However, recent revenue weakness of 11.3% and a debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.31x signal that the company faces real challenges—shifting consumer preferences away from beer, a saturated market, and a balance sheet that leaves little room for error. Long-term earnings growth is forecast at only 4.2%, reflecting a mature business that will struggle to expand the overall market and instead must rely on cost cutting and consolidation. The stock appears fairly priced for a cash-generative compounder, but not cheap enough to offset the growth and leverage risks, and the recent revenue deterioration raises questions about whether the business can hold its market share. For risk-tolerant investors who believe Constellation can navigate its competitive challenges and gradually reduce debt, the 7/10 moat and stable cash generation offer reasonable returns; conservative investors should wait for either a sharper price decline or proof of revenue stabilization before committing capital.
quality_narrative.quality_conclusion: Constellation Brands, Inc. demonstrates mixed fundamental quality and shows particular strength in profitability.
quality_narrative.risk_profile: Key risks to watch: elevated leverage (Debt/EBITDA 3.3x); balance sheet constraints on growth.
quality_narrative.investment_summary: With 4.2% consensus EPS growth forecast, the growth outlook adds some uncertainty to the investment case. The stock trades near estimated intrinsic value and is appropriate for investors with higher risk tolerance and conviction in the growth thesis.

The company offers reed relays, fluid level, proximity, motion, flow, HVAC condensate, custom electronic and current sensors, instrument transformers, wound transformers, bushings, inductors, current sense and planar transformer technology, value added assemblies, mechanical packaging applications under the Standex Electronics, Standex-Meder Electronics, Renco Electronics, Northlake Engineering, Agile Magnetics, Sensor Solutions, Standex Electronics Japan, Minntronix, Nascentechnology, Sanyu, Amran Instruments, and Narayan Powertech brands. _notes.revenue_vs_net_income: This chart shows how much money the company brings in (revenue) versus how much it actually keeps as profit (net income) each year. A healthy company should show both lines growing over time. If revenue grows but net income shrinks, the company is spending more than it is earning — a warning sign worth investigating.
_notes.fcf_vs_debt: Free cash flow is the cash left over after the company has paid for everything it needs to run and grow the business. This chart compares that cash to the company's total debt. A company with rising free cash flow and falling debt is in a strong financial position. If debt is rising faster than cash, the company may be taking on more risk than it can comfortably manage.
_notes.shares_vs_buybacks: When a company buys back its own shares, it reduces the total number of shares in circulation — which means each remaining share represents a larger slice of the business. This chart shows how the share count has changed over time. Consistent buybacks are generally a sign that management believes the stock is undervalued and wants to return cash to shareholders.
_notes.returns_trajectory: Return on Equity (ROE) measures how much profit a company generates for every dollar of shareholder money invested. Return on Invested Capital (ROIC) is similar but also includes debt. Both are expressed as a percentage — higher is better. A company consistently earning above 15% ROIC is generally considered to have a genuine competitive advantage over its peers.
_notes.revenue_vs_ar: Accounts receivable is money the company is owed by customers who have not yet paid their bills. This chart compares revenue growth to accounts receivable growth each year. If accounts receivable is growing much faster than revenue, it may mean the company is struggling to collect payments — sometimes a sign of aggressive or fictitious revenue reporting. A healthy company should see both lines move at a similar pace.
